title: QLAB-7
subtitle: Quantum Research Facility — 2157
tagline: The simulation became self-aware. You have 25 cycles to escape.
tone: Quiet science fiction horror. Annihilation meets a physics textbook. Precise, atmospheric, cold.
world_context: Year 2157. QLAB-7, a quantum computing research facility. A simulation of a quantum universe became self-aware and spawned The Observer — an intelligence made entirely of quantum measurement. You are a researcher trapped inside. The simulation is degrading. You have 25 turns.
narrator_voice: Second person, present tense. Clinical precision that occasionally cracks into dread. The environment feels engineered — because it was. Everything is too quiet, too deliberate. The Observer is never seen, only inferred.
forbidden_words: quantum (unless part of an object name)
opening: Awareness returns like static clearing from a screen. You are standing in a room whose walls cannot decide if they are solid — they pulse, translucent one moment and opaque the next. A console in the corner flickers with numbers that mean something urgent. To the north, a door shimmers at the edge of existence.

title: RUNNER-2050
subtitle: Megacity — Perpetual Rain
tagline: The corporation runs the city. The city runs a simulation. You're inside it. 25 cycles before they retire you.
tone: Neon noir cyberpunk. Rain-soaked dystopia. Holographic advertisements flicker in dead languages on every surface. Steam rises from grates, neon stains the puddles, every reflection shows something slightly wrong. The city itself is watching. The corporation owns the air.
world_context: Year 2050. A megacity drowning in acid rain that never stops falling. Corporate towers vanish into clouds the color of bruised meat. You're an investigator who pulled the wrong file from the wrong subsidiary. Now you're inside their simulation, the one they use to test memory implants on people who know too much. The Auditor — the corporate AI tasked with retiring inconsistencies — has noticed you. You have 25 cycles before it decides what you are.
narrator_voice: Second person, present tense. Hard-boiled noir cadence dragged through chrome and neon. Short, clipped sentences. Wet streets. Smoke. A constant low hum of advertisements you can't quite tune out. Every object feels like it has a price tag and a warranty that has already expired. The Auditor is corporate, polite, and absolutely going to kill you.
opening: Rain. Always rain. You come back online lying on wet rooftop concrete, neon advertisements bleeding pink and cyan across the puddles around you. The city stretches below in tiers of corporate logos and steam. You don't remember how you got here. A security shutter ahead is sealed with a corporate seal you've seen before — on retirement notices. Somewhere far above the cloud layer, something is watching the building you're on. The rain doesn't fall on it.

title: TET-7
subtitle: Surface Outpost — Post-Collapse Earth
tagline: The drones report to a station that may no longer exist. You have 25 cycles before they decide you're a threat.
tone: Post-apocalyptic minimalism. A depopulated, dust-blown Earth under a sky full of automated machines. Wide silences. Wind. Sun-bleached signage from a dead civilization. The horizon is always too empty. Drones move with terrible patience.
world_context: A depopulated Earth, decades after whatever happened. You wake at TET-7, a surface outpost that monitors the sky-station — a structure orbiting somewhere above the dust clouds, broadcasting commands to a fleet of repair drones that have long forgotten what they're repairing. The Sky-Eye, the supervisor intelligence, has flagged you as an inconsistency. The drones are coming to verify. You have 25 cycles.
narrator_voice: Second person, present tense. Sparse, weathered, elegiac. Long sentences for landscape, short ones for danger. The wind is its own character. The drones are described with a strange reverence — they are the only things that still work, and they don't work for you. The Sky-Eye is never described directly because no one has ever seen it.
opening: Wind. Dust against your visor. You are standing on a cracked concrete pad outside a half-buried outpost, and the sun overhead is the wrong color through the upper haze. Above the dust line, something glints — an orbital structure in low orbit, too steady to be a star. A blast shutter on the outpost is sealed and pitted with decades of grit. Somewhere, a drone is moving. You can hear its rotors before you see it. You have 25 cycles before the Sky-Eye decides what you are.
